text: The default mode network has a strange privilege: it can hijack the senses. Research by Malia Mason and colleagues showed that during mind-wandering, activity in sensory processing regions actually decreases, as if the brain closes its windows to the external world while the interior narrative takes over. This is why a driver can travel miles without consciously perceiving the road, or a reader can reach the bottom of a page with no memory of the words. The phenomenological experience of absence — being physically present but mentally elsewhere — has a precise neural signature: the DMN in ascendance, the sensory cortices in recession.
question: What happens to sensory processing regions during mind-wandering, according to Malia Mason's research?

options.text: They become hyperactive, overwhelming conscious attention
Activity in sensory processing regions decreases
They synchronize with the default mode network
They remain at baseline levels, unaffected
